title: Japanese tissue paper mending
subtitle: Archival repair of paper tears and holes using kozo tissue
excerpt: Japanese tissue repair is the conservation standard for torn, weakened, or holed paper. A thin strip of kozo tissue, wetted with dilute paste and laid over the damage, dries transparent and adds strength without stiffness. The technique works on paper manuscripts, drawings, and book leaves.
sourceNotes: Conservation practice as documented in Christopher Clarkson, Paper Conservation: A Guide to Conservation and Restoration of Books and Paper (1993, Society of Bookbinders). The water-tear method for kozo strips is standard conservation technique taught in book conservation training.

glossaryTerms.definition: A technique for tearing Japanese tissue along a damp line to produce a soft feathered edge, which blends into the parent paper more invisibly than a cut edge.
A dilute adhesive (usually wheat-paste or methyl cellulose) used to stabilise fragile or flaking paper before or during repair.

content.text: The principle of Japanese tissue repair is minimal intervention: the thinnest possible patch, applied reversibly, using an adhesive that can be released with moisture. Kozo tissue from Japan is made from paper-mulberry bark fibres; even at 3-5 gsm it has considerable tensile strength, and its long fibres lock into the parent paper's surface. This tutorial repairs a simple straight tear and a small hole.

items.name: Kozo Japanese tissue, 3-5 gsm (the thinnest available)
items.qty: One small sheet, a 20 cm square is enough for most book-leaf repairs

items.name: Wheat-paste (cooked, diluted 1:3 with water)
items.qty: A tablespoon in a small dish

items.name: Small spray bottle of clean water

attrs.body: Work on a clean glass plate. Kozo tissue is so fine it is nearly invisible when wet; lay it on a dark surface or use a light box underneath to see what you're doing.

content.text: Tear the repair strip using the

rather than cutting: draw a damp brush or wet finger in a line on the tissue, wait 10 seconds, then pull gently apart along the wet line. The feathered edge from a water tear blends into the parent paper surface; a cut edge stands up as a visible ridge when the repair dries. Cut strips only for very confined repairs where a soft edge would extend too far.

Lay the damaged leaf face-down on clean blotting paper. Align the torn edges so they butt together; they may need a tiny amount of moisture (spray mist) to relax flat. Paint dilute paste over the tear line and a few millimetres either side. Lay the tissue strip face-down over the paste, overlapping both edges of the tear. Pat gently with a damp finger to bed the tissue into the paste. Cover with a clean sheet of blotting paper, then a flat board and light weight. Leave to dry for 30 minutes.

Tear a tissue patch slightly larger than the hole. Paste the area around the hole, then from behind the paper lower the tissue patch over the hole, ensuring the patch extends at least 5 mm beyond the hole edge on all sides. Smooth from the centre outward. For a hole with clean edges, apply a second small patch on the face side, positioned the same way. Press and dry as for a tear.
The same technique consolidates fragile paper before rebinding: a Japanese tissue

layer, brushed over the entire spine area of a crumbling text block, stabilises the paper enough to handle for resewing. Heat-set tissue (tissue backed with a reversible adhesive activated by a warm iron) is a faster alternative for book-conservation centres; the cold-paste method taught here is the reversible hand-conservation version.
